title: SEGMENT 7 — Directed evolution in service of metabolic pathways

lines.text: SEGMENT 7 — Directed evolution in service of metabolic pathways
So you have a pathway.
You have installed it.
You have used FBA to predict the manipulations that should push flux into your product.
You run the strain.
The yield is, charitably, a tenth of what you predicted.
Why?
Often because one or more of the heterologous enzymes — the plant or fungal enzymes you transplanted into your microbial host — is performing at a fraction of its native activity.
The cell's solvent is wrong.
The cofactor pool is wrong.
The substrate pool is wrong.
The enzyme is folding badly.
Whatever the reason, the enzyme is the bottleneck, and you cannot fix it by adjusting fermentation conditions.
You need to make the enzyme itself better.
The dominant tool for this is directed evolution, and we owe its modern form largely to Frances Arnold, who shared the 2018 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for it.

The idea is to do, in a few weeks at the lab bench, what natural evolution would take millions of years to do — generate variants of an enzyme, screen them for the property you want, keep the winners, repeat.
The cycle is mutate, screen, select, repeat.
Mutating is the easy part.
You can do error-prone PCR, where you deliberately use a low-fidelity polymerase to introduce random errors into your gene at a controlled rate, generating a library of variants.
You can do DNA shuffling, where you take homologous genes from related organisms, chop them up, and reassemble them, generating chimeric variants that mix and match domains.
You can do site-saturation mutagenesis, where you target specific residues — for example, residues lining the active site — and generate every possible amino acid substitution at each one.
The output is a library: a few thousand to a few million variants of your gene, each carried in a separate cell.
The hard part is screening.
You need a way to look at each variant and ask, is this one better than the parent?
For some properties — fluorescence, antibiotic resistance, growth on a particular substrate — screening is straightforward.

For more exotic chemistry — does this enzyme convert substrate A to product B more efficiently — you often have to invent a custom screen, sometimes by linking the chemistry you care about to a fluorescent or growth readout.
There is a whole subculture of clever biosensor design within directed evolution that exists exclusively to make screens feasible.
Sometimes you can do high-throughput screening with robotics, running thousands of variants through an assay per day.

Sometimes you can do selection, where the variants that have the property you want simply outgrow the variants that do not — fastest, most powerful, but only works when the property is tied to survival.
The interplay of directed evolution and metabolic engineering is intimate.
Almost every successful industrial pathway has had some of its enzymes evolved for the production context.
The artemisinin yeast went through rounds of enzyme evolution.
The opioid yeast we will discuss in Segment 12 required extensive enzyme engineering of borrowed plant P450s.
The biofuels work at Amyris and at LS9 used directed evolution heavily.
The pattern is: install the pathway in the host, run it, identify which step is the bottleneck, evolve that step, repeat.
You essentially do strain engineering at the level of the whole cell and enzyme engineering at the level of the individual proteins, in parallel, iteratively.

The analogy I want you to carry: directed evolution is the engineer's gift of accelerated evolution — millions of years of selection compressed into weeks of screening — applied to an enzyme that needs to work in a context evolution never prepared it for.
You take the enzyme, mutate it, find the variants that work better in your cellular context, and the cell now has a tool that fits its new floor plan.
Now, the next layer of complexity.
Even if you have all your enzymes working beautifully, the pathway may still fail because the cofactors are wrong.
Cofactors are the small molecules that enzymes use as helpers — and the balance of cofactors is one of the chronic battlefields of metabolic engineering.
